text: Following the tunneling and dissection of the superior mesenteric vein, the surgeon performs lateral dissection of the portal vein, maintaining proximity to the superior mesenteric vein rather than the pancreas.
The surgeon applies constant traction to the duodenum in the supracolic region, as visually demonstrated in the video.
The surgeon performs the cattle brush maneuver to fully expose the superior mesenteric vein, ensuring clear visualization of the vein's anatomical course.

The assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein and mesentery to the left, while the surgeon retracts the duodenum to the right within the supracolic compartment, creating a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the dissection to remove the entire mesopancreas, focusing on separating it from surrounding structures.
The video shows the origin or insertion of the first jejunal vein into the superior mesenteric vein, highlighted by a yellow arrow.
The surgeon identifies the first jejunal vein, a critical step in the dissection of the mesopancreas, with the vein visibly highlighted in the surgical field.
The first jejunal vein is visible, draining blood from the jejunal loop and passing behind the superior mesenteric artery before inserting into the superior mesenteric vein.
The first jejunal branch is identified, with the assistant providing retraction to enhance visibility of the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the superior mesenteric vein to the left, exposing the first jejunal branch. Concurrently, the duodenum and jejunal loop are retracted to the left side within the supracolic compartment.
The surgeon performs dissection above the superior mesenteric artery, focusing on the region where the first jejunal vein is identified and its relationship with the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon retracts the tissue to expose the first jejunal vein, which is highlighted by a yellow arrow for clear identification.
The surgeon maneuvers the superior mesenteric artery to the right of the superior mesenteric vein, adjusting the spatial orientation of these vessels.
The surgeon performs suction dissection directly over the superior mesenteric artery, with visible retraction of surrounding tissues to enhance exposure.
The first regional vein is visibly reinserted into the superior mesenteric vein, with the superior mesenteric artery located right behind it, displaying noticeable pulsations.
The surgeon ligates and cuts the tributaries of the first jejunal vein, carefully dissecting these smaller vessels without sacrificing the main vein.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric artery, visible with pulsations, located just behind the branch of the first jejunal vein. The first jejunal vein is also clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue, causing it to kink and rotate behind the superior mesenteric artery, then move above and descend behind it, visually hooking around the artery.
The surgeon identifies the first jejunal vein and continues dissection either underneath it or above the superior mesenteric artery, as visible in the current video.
The surgeon continues dissection above the superior mesenteric artery, ensuring complete removal of the mesopancreas, with visible anatomical structures including the artery and surrounding tissue.

The surgeon retracts the specimen to the left side of the screen while the assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein or portal vein to the right, exposing the mesopancreas or pancreatic mesentery for further dissection.
The pancreatic mesentery is clearly visible lying directly on top of the superior mesenteric artery, with surrounding anatomical structures exposed.

The surgeon and assistant maintain traction on the specimen and the superior mesenteric vein, respectively, to expose the superior mesenteric artery. The first jejunal branch vein is identified in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips the tributaries around the superior mesenteric artery, with the first jejunal branch visibly hooking around the artery.
The surgeon completes the removal of the entire mesopancreas, ensuring it is fully excised from the surgical field.
The surgical team has reached the retroperitoneum, with the pancreatic parenchyma fully dissected and included in the specimen, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon and assistant apply traction in opposite directions, causing the superior mesenteric artery, initially positioned to the left of the superior mesenteric vein, to shift to the right side of the vein.
The surgeon elongates the mesopancreas, exposing the uncinate process for complete resection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to precisely dissect tissue adjacent to the superior mesenteric artery, which is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the superior mesenteric artery located behind the first jejunal vein, emphasizing the importance of clearly visualizing the first jejunal branch to facilitate the dissection and identification process.
The surgeon navigates along the superior mesenteric artery, encountering the superior pancreaticoduodenal vein, which visibly enters into the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the mesopancreas, ensuring clear identification of anatomical structures to facilitate the dissection around the superior mesenteric vein.
